name: South Vietnam (1948-1975)

colors.symbolism: the imperial colour of the Vietnamese throne and the unity of the Vietnamese people
the three regions of Vietnam — Tonkin (north), Annam (centre) and Cochinchina (south); also the blood of patriots

description: A yellow field crossed horizontally at the centre by three thin red stripes (running the full width, separated by yellow stripes of equal width).
history: Adopted on 2 June 1948 as the flag of the State of Vietnam established under former emperor Bao Dai, succeeding the various provisional flags of the Indochina War period. The 'three-line' design derived from the imperial flag of the Nguyen dynasty (the 1890 Long Tinh and the wartime Quẻ Ly variants), with three red stripes representing the three regions of Vietnam — Tonkin, Annam, and Cochinchina — on the yellow imperial field. The flag was retained by the Republic of Vietnam (South Vietnam) after the 1954 partition at the 17th parallel and through the Vietnam War. It was retired on 30 April 1975 with the Fall of Saigon and the unification of Vietnam under the present red flag of the Socialist Republic of Vietnam in 1976. It remains in widespread use among the Vietnamese diaspora as the 'Heritage and Freedom' flag.

features.symbolism: three red horizontal stripes on a yellow field — the 'three-line' (Co Vang Ba Soc) flag drawn from the historic colours of the Nguyen dynasty
the three traditional regions of Vietnam (Bac Ky / Trung Ky / Nam Ky), unified under one yellow imperial banner
